era_context: Late 12th Dynasty Egypt, fragmented Mesopotamian city-states, mature Indus Valley Civilization, and the traditionally dated Xia Dynasty in China. This period is characterized by regional consolidation, palace economies, and extensive long-distance trade networks. Written documentation is sparse outside Egypt.

events.title: Egyptian 12th Dynasty administration and building projects
events.description: The 12th Dynasty was a period of intensive building and administrative consolidation. Temples, pyramids, and administrative centers were constructed throughout Egypt. The Labyrinth at Hawara, attributed to Amenemhat III, represents the apex of Middle Kingdom architectural ambition. Papyri from this period document a sophisticated bureaucracy managing agriculture, taxation, and trade. However, no specific events are recorded for this particular year.
key_figures: Amenemhat III (Pharaoh of Egypt, if still reigning)

sources.name: Archaeological remains of 12th Dynasty temples and administrative buildings

events.certainty_note: The 12th Dynasty chronology is uncertain by ±30 years. Assignment to this year is conventional.

disconfirming_evidence: No year-specific events are documented for -1922 in any surviving source. The assignment of this year to the 12th Dynasty is based on conventional chronologies that are themselves disputed. Some scholars place the 12th Dynasty earlier or later by several decades.
historiographic_note: This year is essentially undocumented in surviving sources. All statements are generalizations from the broader 12th Dynasty period. The Egyptian chronology for this period is contested, with 'high' and 'low' chronologies differing by up to 100 years. No other civilization has written records for this specific year. This is a typical situation for the early second millennium BCE outside of Egypt.
